topics.title: What is Python?
topics.content:
What is Python?

Python is a beginner-friendly, high-level programming language created by Guido van Rossum in 1991. It is one of the most popular programming languages in the world, used in web development, data science, artificial intelligence, and automation.

Why Python?

  • Clean, readable syntax — almost like English
  • Massive ecosystem of libraries (NumPy, Pandas, Django, etc.)
  • Used at Google, Instagram, NASA, and Netflix
  • Perfect as a first programming language
💡 Python uses indentation (4 spaces or 1 tab) to define code blocks — not curly braces like most languages.

topics.title: Your First Python Program
topics.content:
Hello, World!

By tradition, the first program you write in any language prints "Hello, World!". In Python it takes exactly one line:

print("Hello, World!")

Run it and you will see:

Hello, World!

How print() works

The print() function displays any value to the screen. You can print text (strings), numbers, or even the result of calculations:

print("Welcome to Python!")
print(42)
print(3 + 7)
💡 Strings (text) must be wrapped in quotes — either single ' or double ".

topics.title: Comments
topics.content:
Writing Comments

Comments are notes in your code that Python ignores when running. They help you and others understand what the code does.

# This is a single-line comment
print("Hello!")  # This comment is at the end of a line

"""
This is a multi-line comment
(actually a docstring, but commonly used as a comment)
"""

Why write comments?

  • Explain why you wrote the code a certain way
  • Leave notes for your future self
  • Temporarily disable code while testing
💡 Good programmers comment their code. Aim for comments that explain why, not what — the code itself shows what.

evalQuestions.question: Which function is used to display output in Python?
evalQuestions.hint: Think about the built-in function you use every time you want to show something on screen.

evalQuestions.question: How do you write a single-line comment in Python?
evalQuestions.hint: Python uses a symbol that looks like a hashtag.

evalQuestions.question: What will the following code print?
evalQuestions.hint: Evaluate the arithmetic expression inside print().

topics.title: Variables
topics.content:
Storing Data in Variables

A variable is a named container that stores data. In Python you create a variable simply by assigning a value — no keyword needed.

name = "Maria"   # text
age  = 20        # whole number
gpa  = 1.75      # decimal
is_enrolled = True  # yes/no

Naming Rules

  • Start with a letter or underscore (_)
  • Can contain letters, numbers, and underscores
  • Case-sensitive: Name ≠ name
  • Cannot use Python keywords (if, for, while, etc.)
💡 Python convention: use snake_case — lowercase words joined by underscores: student_name, total_score.

topics.title: Data Types
topics.content:
The Four Core Data Types

TypeExampleUse case
str"hello"Text and characters
int42Whole numbers
float3.14Decimal numbers
boolTrue / FalseLogic / yes-no values

Checking the Type

x = 42
print(type(x))      # <class 'int'>
print(type(3.14))   # <class 'float'>
print(type("hi"))   # <class 'str'>
print(type(True))   # <class 'bool'>

Type Conversion

print(int("42"))    # 42   str → int
print(float(5))     # 5.0  int → float
print(str(100))     # "100" int → str

topics.title: Operators
topics.content:
Arithmetic Operators

print(10 + 3)   # 13  — addition
print(10 - 3)   # 7   — subtraction
print(10 * 3)   # 30  — multiplication
print(10 / 3)   # 3.333… — division (always float)
print(10 // 3)  # 3   — floor division (drops decimal)
print(10 % 3)   # 1   — modulo (remainder)
print(2 ** 8)   # 256 — exponentiation

Comparison Operators

print(5 == 5)   # True
print(5 != 3)   # True
print(5 > 3)    # True
print(5 <= 3)   # False
⚠️ == checks equality; = assigns. Never confuse them!

evalQuestions.question: Which is the correct way to create a variable in Python?
evalQuestions.hint: Python does not require a keyword to declare a variable.

evalQuestions.question: What data type does Python assign to the value 3.14?
evalQuestions.hint: Numbers with decimal points are a specific numeric type.

evalQuestions.question: Fill in the blank to convert the string '42' to an integer.
evalQuestions.hint: Use the built-in function that converts to integer.

evalQuestions.question: What is the output of the following code?
evalQuestions.hint: % gives the remainder after division.

topics.title: if / elif / else
topics.content:
Making Decisions

The if statement runs code only when a condition is true.

grade = int(input("Enter grade: "))

if grade >= 90:
    print("Excellent!")
elif grade >= 75:
    print("Passed")
elif grade >= 60:
    print("Conditional Passed")
else:
    print("Failed")

Logical Operators

age = 20
is_enrolled = True

if age >= 18 and is_enrolled:
    print("Eligible")

if age < 18 or not is_enrolled:
    print("Not eligible")
💡 Python uses and, or, not — not &&, ||, !.

topics.title: for Loops
topics.content:
Repeating with for

A for loop iterates over a sequence — a range of numbers, a list, or any iterable.

# Loop 5 times
for i in range(5):
    print(i)        # prints 0 1 2 3 4

# Loop from 1 to 5
for i in range(1, 6):
    print(i)        # prints 1 2 3 4 5

# Loop over a list
fruits = ["apple", "mango", "banana"]
for fruit in fruits:
    print(fruit)

Loop Control

for i in range(10):
    if i == 5:
        break       # exit the loop
    if i % 2 == 0:
        continue    # skip even numbers
    print(i)        # prints 1 3

topics.title: while Loops
topics.content:
Repeating with while

A while loop keeps running as long as a condition is True.

count = 0
while count < 5:
    print("count:", count)
    count += 1      # Don't forget to update the condition!

# Output: count: 0  count: 1  count: 2  count: 3  count: 4

Infinite Loop Warning

# ⚠️ This runs forever — never do this!
# while True:
#     print("forever")
⚠️ Always make sure a while loop's condition will eventually become False, or use break to exit.

evalQuestions.question: Which is the correct syntax for an if statement in Python?
evalQuestions.hint: Python uses a colon, not brackets or 'then'.

evalQuestions.question: What does range(1, 5) produce?
evalQuestions.hint: range(start, stop) includes start but excludes stop.

evalQuestions.question: Arrange the lines to create a for loop that prints numbers 1 to 3.
evalQuestions.hint: The for statement comes first, then the print inside the loop.

topics.title: Defining Functions
topics.content:
Reusable Code Blocks

Functions let you write code once and call it many times.

def greet(name):
    print(f"Hello, {name}!")

greet("Maria")   # Hello, Maria!
greet("Juan")    # Hello, Juan!

Return Values

def add(a, b):
    return a + b

result = add(3, 7)
print(result)   # 10

Default Parameters

def greet(name, greeting="Hello"):
    print(f"{greeting}, {name}!")

greet("Maria")           # Hello, Maria!
greet("Juan", "Kamusta") # Kamusta, Juan!
💡 Each function should do one thing. Keep them short and focused.

topics.title: Lists
topics.content:
Working with Lists

A list stores multiple values in an ordered, changeable sequence.

fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]

print(fruits[0])    # apple (first element)
print(fruits[-1])   # cherry (last element)
print(len(fruits))  # 3

# Modifying
fruits.append("mango")   # add to end
fruits.remove("banana")  # remove by value
fruits.pop()             # remove and return last
fruits.sort()            # sort alphabetically

List Slicing

nums = [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
print(nums[1:4])    # [1, 2, 3]
print(nums[:3])     # [0, 1, 2]
print(nums[3:])     # [3, 4, 5]

topics.title: Input & Output
topics.content:
Getting User Input

name = input("Enter your name: ")
print(f"Hello, {name}!")
⚠️ input() always returns a string. Convert it if you need a number:
age = int(input("Enter your age: "))
height = float(input("Height in meters: "))

print(f"In 5 years you will be {age + 5}.")

f-Strings (Formatted Strings)

name = "Maria"
score = 95
print(f"Student: {name}, Score: {score}")
# Student: Maria, Score: 95

evalQuestions.question: What keyword is used to define a function in Python?
evalQuestions.hint: It is a short form of 'define'.

evalQuestions.question: Fill in the blank to add 'mango' to the end of the list.
evalQuestions.hint: Which list method adds an element to the end?

evalQuestions.question: Arrange the lines to define a function that returns the sum of two numbers, then call it.
evalQuestions.hint: Define the function before calling it.

evalQuestions.question: What is the output of the following code?
evalQuestions.hint: sum() adds all elements of a list.

topics.title: Dictionaries
topics.content:
Key-Value Storage

A dictionary stores data as key-value pairs. Use it when data has named attributes.

student = {
    "name": "Maria",
    "age": 20,
    "gpa": 1.75
}

print(student["name"])              # Maria
print(student.get("grade", "N/A")) # N/A (safe access)

student["year"] = 2    # add new key
del student["gpa"]     # remove key

# Iterating
for key, value in student.items():
    print(f"{key}: {value}")

topics.title: List Comprehensions
topics.content:
Elegant List Building

List comprehensions create lists in a single readable line.

# Traditional
squares = []
for x in range(5):
    squares.append(x**2)

# List comprehension (same result)
squares = [x**2 for x in range(5)]
print(squares)   # [0, 1, 4, 9, 16]

# With condition
evens = [x for x in range(10) if x % 2 == 0]
print(evens)     # [0, 2, 4, 6, 8]

evalQuestions.question: What is a Python dictionary?
evalQuestions.hint: Think about how you look something up by name.

evalQuestions.question: What is the output of the following code?
evalQuestions.hint: dict.get(key, default) returns default if key is not found.

evalQuestions.question: What does [x**2 for x in range(5)] produce?
evalQuestions.hint: range(5) starts at 0.

topics.title: Classes and Objects
topics.content:
Object-Oriented Programming

OOP organises code around objects — bundles of data (attributes) and behaviour (methods).

class Student:
    school = "CodeArena"   # class attribute

    def __init__(self, name, age):
        self.name = name   # instance attribute
        self.age  = age

    def greet(self):
        print(f"Hi, I am {self.name}!")

s = Student("Maria", 20)
s.greet()   # Hi, I am Maria!

Inheritance

class GradStudent(Student):
    def __init__(self, name, age, thesis):
        super().__init__(name, age)
        self.thesis = thesis

topics.title: Error Handling
topics.content:
Handling Exceptions

try:
    age = int(input("Enter age: "))
    print(f"Next year: {age + 1}")
except ValueError:
    print("Please enter a valid number.")
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Unexpected error: {e}")
else:
    print("No errors occurred!")
finally:
    print("This always runs.")

Common Exceptions

  • ValueError — invalid value (e.g. int("abc"))
  • TypeError — wrong type
  • IndexError — list index out of range
  • KeyError — missing dictionary key
  • ZeroDivisionError — dividing by zero

evalQuestions.question: What does the __init__ method do in a class?
options: Initialises instance attributes when an object is created
evalQuestions.hint: It is called automatically when you create a new object.

evalQuestions.question: Which exception is raised when dividing by zero?
evalQuestions.hint: The exception name describes exactly what happened.

evalQuestions.question: Fill in the correct exception type for invalid integer conversion.
evalQuestions.hint: int('abc') raises a specific exception related to the value.

evalQuestions.question: Arrange the lines to define a Person class and call its greet method.
evalQuestions.hint: Define the class and its methods before creating an instance.

topics.title: Generators
topics.content:
Memory-Efficient Sequences

Generators produce values one at a time using yield, without building a full list in memory.

def countdown(n):
    while n > 0:
        yield n
        n -= 1

for val in countdown(3):
    print(val)   # 3, 2, 1

Generator Expressions

# List (all in memory)
squares_list = [x**2 for x in range(1000000)]

# Generator (lazy, efficient)
squares_gen = (x**2 for x in range(1000000))
💡 Use generators when you work with large data sets or infinite sequences — they are far more memory-efficient than lists.

topics.title: Decorators
topics.content:
Wrapping Functions

A decorator wraps a function to add behaviour without modifying its code.

def my_decorator(func):
    def wrapper():
        print("Before")
        func()
        print("After")
    return wrapper

@my_decorator
def say_hello():
    print("Hello!")

say_hello()
# Before
# Hello!
# After

Practical: Timing Decorator

import time

def timer(func):
    def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
        start = time.time()
        result = func(*args, **kwargs)
        print(f"Elapsed: {time.time()-start:.4f}s")
        return result
    return wrapper

evalQuestions.question: What makes a function a generator in Python?
evalQuestions.hint: Generators produce values one at a time.

evalQuestions.question: What is the output of the following code?
evalQuestions.hint: Trace what the decorator wraps around the original call.

evalQuestions.question: Fill in the blank to complete the generator.
evalQuestions.hint: What keyword makes a function a generator?

topics.title: Introduction to JavaScript
topics.content:
What is JavaScript?

JavaScript is the programming language of the web. It runs in every browser and lets you make pages interactive — responding to clicks, fetching data, and updating content without a page reload.

Where does JavaScript run?

  • Browser — client-side interactivity (animations, forms, games)
  • Server — Node.js powers backends like Discord and Netflix
  • Mobile — React Native apps

Hello, World!

console.log("Hello, World!");

topics.title: Variables and Data Types
topics.content:
Storing Data

let name    = "Maria";    // string
let age     = 20;          // number
let gpa     = 1.75;        // number (float)
let enrolled = true;       // boolean
const PI    = 3.14159;     // constant — cannot reassign

var vs let vs const

KeywordScopeReassignable
varfunction✓
letblock✓
constblock✗
💡 Prefer const by default; use let when you need to reassign. Avoid var.

topics.title: Functions & Arrays
topics.content:
Functions

// Function declaration
function greet(name) {
  return `Hello, ${name}!`;
}

// Arrow function (modern)
const square = n => n * n;

console.log(greet("Maria"));  // Hello, Maria!
console.log(square(5));        // 25

Arrays

const fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"];
console.log(fruits[0]);           // apple
console.log(fruits.length);       // 3

fruits.push("mango");             // add to end
fruits.pop();                     // remove last

const evens = [1,2,3,4].filter(n => n % 2 === 0);
console.log(evens);               // [2, 4]

evalQuestions.question: Which keyword declares a block-scoped variable in modern JavaScript?
evalQuestions.hint: Introduced in ES6, it replaced var for most use cases.

evalQuestions.question: What does === check in JavaScript?
evalQuestions.hint: It is stricter than ==.

evalQuestions.question: What is the output of the following code?
evalQuestions.hint: += adds to the current value.

evalQuestions.question: Arrange the lines to define and call a function that squares a number.
evalQuestions.hint: Declare the function before calling it.

topics.title: What is Python?
topics.content:
What is Python?

Python is a high-level, interpreted programming language known for its simple and readable syntax. It was created by Guido van Rossum and first released in 1991.

Python is used in many fields:

  • 🌐 Web development (Django, Flask)
  • 🤖 Artificial Intelligence & Machine Learning
  • 📊 Data Science & Analytics
  • 🔧 Automation & Scripting
  • 🎮 Game Development

Why Python?

Python's philosophy emphasizes code readability. Compare a simple greeting in different languages:

# Python — clean and simple
print("Hello, World!")

Python reads almost like plain English, making it one of the best languages for beginners.

topics.title: Your First Python Program
topics.content:
Hello, World!

Every programming journey starts with a "Hello, World!" program. In Python, it's just one line:

print("Hello, World!")

The print() function outputs text to the screen. You can print any text by placing it inside the parentheses, enclosed in quotes.

Try More Examples

print("My name is Python!")
print("I am learning to code.")
print("2 + 2 =", 2 + 2)

Output:

My name is Python!
I am learning to code.
2 + 2 = 4

Comments

Comments are notes in your code that Python ignores. Use the # symbol:

# This is a comment — Python ignores it
print("This runs!")  # You can also comment after code

topics.title: Python Syntax Basics
topics.content:
Indentation

Python uses indentation (spaces or tabs) to define code blocks. This is mandatory — unlike other languages that use curly braces.

# Correct indentation
if True:
    print("This is indented correctly")
    print("Same block")

# Wrong — will cause an IndentationError
if True:
print("This will fail")  # Missing indent!

Case Sensitivity

Python is case-sensitive. name, Name, and NAME are three different variables.

name = "Alice"
Name = "Bob"
print(name)  # Alice
print(Name)  # Bob

Line Endings

Each statement in Python goes on its own line. You don't need semicolons (though they're allowed).

x = 5
y = 10
z = x + y
print(z)  # 15

evalQuestions.question: Which function is used to display output in Python?
What symbol is used to write a comment in Python?
Python was created by ___.
What will `print(2 + 3)` output?

topics.title: Variables
topics.content:
What is a Variable?

A variable is a named container that stores a value. Think of it as a labeled box that holds data.

name = "Alice"      # Stores a string
age  = 20           # Stores an integer
height = 5.6        # Stores a decimal number
is_student = True   # Stores a boolean

Variable Naming Rules

  • Must start with a letter or underscore (_)
  • Can contain letters, numbers, and underscores
  • Cannot use reserved keywords (if, for, while, etc.)
  • Case-sensitive: score ≠ Score
# Good variable names
student_name = "Bob"
_private_var = 42
totalScore = 95

# Bad variable names (will cause errors)
2fast = 100      # starts with a number
my-var = "hi"   # hyphens not allowed

topics.title: Data Types
topics.content:
Python's Core Data Types

Python has several built-in data types. The most common ones are:

  • int — whole numbers: 5, -3, 100
  • float — decimal numbers: 3.14, -0.5
  • str — text (strings): "hello", 'Python'
  • bool — True or False
x = 42          # int
y = 3.14        # float
z = "Python"    # str
flag = True     # bool

# Check the type
print(type(x))    # <class 'int'>
print(type(y))    # <class 'float'>
print(type(z))    # <class 'str'>
print(type(flag)) # <class 'bool'>

topics.title: Type Conversion
topics.content:
Converting Between Types

You can convert one data type to another using built-in functions:

  • int() — converts to integer
  • float() — converts to float
  • str() — converts to string
  • bool() — converts to boolean
x = "42"          # This is a string
y = int(x)        # Now it's an integer: 42
z = float(x)      # Now it's a float: 42.0
w = str(100)      # Now it's a string: "100"

print(y + 8)      # 50  (math works now!)
print(w + " pts") # "100 pts"

Common Mistake

age = input("Enter your age: ")  # input() always returns a string!
age = int(age)                    # Convert to int before doing math
next_year = age + 1
print("Next year you'll be", next_year)

evalQuestions.question: Which data type would store the value 3.14?
What does `type(True)` return?
What will `int("7") + 3` evaluate to?
What function is used to convert a value to a string?

topics.title: Arithmetic Operators
topics.content:
Arithmetic Operators

Python supports all standard arithmetic operations:

a = 10
b = 3

print(a + b)   # 13  — Addition
print(a - b)   # 7   — Subtraction
print(a * b)   # 30  — Multiplication
print(a / b)   # 3.333... — Division (always float)
print(a // b)  # 3   — Floor Division (integer result)
print(a % b)   # 1   — Modulus (remainder)
print(a ** b)  # 1000 — Exponentiation (10³)

Order of Operations

Python follows standard math rules (PEMDAS): Parentheses → Exponents → Multiply/Divide → Add/Subtract.

result = 2 + 3 * 4      # 14  (not 20)
result = (2 + 3) * 4   # 20  (parentheses first)

topics.title: Comparison & Logical Operators
topics.content:
Comparison Operators

These operators compare two values and return True or False:

x = 10
y = 5

print(x == y)  # False — Equal to
print(x != y)  # True  — Not equal to
print(x > y)   # True  — Greater than
print(x < y)   # False — Less than
print(x >= 10) # True  — Greater than or equal
print(x <= 10) # True  — Less than or equal

Logical Operators

Combine multiple conditions:

age = 20
has_id = True

# and — both must be True
print(age >= 18 and has_id)  # True

# or — at least one must be True
print(age < 18 or has_id)    # True

# not — reverses True/False
print(not has_id)             # False

topics.title: Assignment Operators
topics.content:
Assignment Operators

Beyond simple =, Python has shorthand assignment operators:

score = 100

score += 10   # score = score + 10  → 110
score -= 5    # score = score - 5   → 105
score *= 2    # score = score * 2   → 210
score //= 3   # score = score // 3  → 70
score **= 2   # score = score ** 2  → 4900
score %= 100  # score = score % 100 → 0

print(score)  # 0

These are useful for updating counters and accumulators:

total = 0
total += 10   # Add 10
total += 20   # Add 20
total += 30   # Add 30
print(total)  # 60

evalQuestions.question: What does `10 % 3` evaluate to?
What is `2 ** 4`?
What does `x += 5` mean?
Which operator checks if two values are equal?

topics.title: if Statements
topics.content:
Making Decisions with if

The if statement lets your program make decisions based on conditions:

temperature = 35

if temperature > 30:
    print("It's hot today!")
    print("Wear sunscreen.")

The code inside the if block runs only if the condition is True.

if-else

Use else to handle the case when the condition is False:

score = 65

if score >= 70:
    print("You passed!")
else:
    print("You need more practice.")

topics.title: elif — Multiple Conditions
topics.content:
elif — Else If

Use elif to check multiple conditions in sequence:

grade = 85

if grade >= 90:
    print("A — Excellent!")
elif grade >= 80:
    print("B — Great job!")
elif grade >= 70:
    print("C — Good work.")
elif grade >= 60:
    print("D — Needs improvement.")
else:
    print("F — Please see your teacher.")

Python checks each condition from top to bottom and runs the first block whose condition is True.

Nested if Statements

age = 20
has_ticket = True

if age >= 18:
    if has_ticket:
        print("You may enter.")
    else:
        print("You need a ticket.")
else:
    print("You must be 18 or older.")

topics.title: One-Line Conditions (Ternary)
topics.content:
Inline if Expression

Python has a concise one-line way to write simple if-else:

# Standard way
if score >= 70:
    result = "Pass"
else:
    result = "Fail"

# One-liner (ternary expression)
result = "Pass" if score >= 70 else "Fail"
print(result)

Practical Examples

age = 17
status = "adult" if age >= 18 else "minor"
print(status)  # minor

# Check even or odd
number = 7
parity = "even" if number % 2 == 0 else "odd"
print(parity)  # odd

evalQuestions.question: What keyword is used for a secondary condition check after `if`?

What will this print?
```
x = 10
if x > 5:
    print("big")
else:
    print("small")
```
What does `"even" if n % 2 == 0 else "odd"` evaluate to when n = 4?
In Python, indentation inside an `if` block is ___.

topics.title: while Loop
topics.content:
The while Loop

A while loop repeats a block of code as long as a condition is True:

count = 1

while count <= 5:
    print(count)
    count += 1

# Output: 1 2 3 4 5

Avoiding Infinite Loops

Always make sure your condition will eventually become False, or your loop will run forever!

# Safe: count increases each iteration
count = 0
while count < 3:
    print("Running...")
    count += 1  # Without this, it loops forever!

break and continue

i = 0
while True:
    i += 1
    if i == 3:
        continue  # Skip 3
    if i > 5:
        break     # Stop at 6
    print(i)      # Prints: 1 2 4 5

topics.title: for Loop & range()
topics.content:
The for Loop

A for loop iterates over a sequence (list, string, range, etc.):

# Loop over a range
for i in range(5):
    print(i)  # 0 1 2 3 4

# Loop over a list
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]
for fruit in fruits:
    print(fruit)

range() Function

range(5)        # 0, 1, 2, 3, 4
range(1, 6)     # 1, 2, 3, 4, 5  (start, stop)
range(0, 10, 2) # 0, 2, 4, 6, 8  (start, stop, step)
range(10, 0, -2)# 10, 8, 6, 4, 2 (counting down)

Loop with Index

names = ["Ana", "Ben", "Cara"]
for i, name in enumerate(names):
    print(f"{i}: {name}")
# Output:
# 0: Ana
# 1: Ben
# 2: Cara

topics.title: Nested Loops
topics.content:
Loops Inside Loops

You can place one loop inside another. The inner loop completes all its iterations for each single iteration of the outer loop.

for i in range(1, 4):
    for j in range(1, 4):
        print(f"{i} x {j} = {i * j}")

# Output:
# 1 x 1 = 1
# 1 x 2 = 2
# 1 x 3 = 3
# 2 x 1 = 2
# ... and so on

Multiplication Table

for i in range(1, 6):
    row = ""
    for j in range(1, 6):
        row += f"{i * j:4}"
    print(row)

evalQuestions.question:
How many times does this loop run?
```
for i in range(3):
    print(i)
```
What keyword immediately exits a loop?
What does `range(2, 8, 2)` produce?
Which keyword skips the current iteration and moves to the next?

topics.title: Defining Functions
topics.content:
What is a Function?

A function is a reusable block of code that performs a specific task. Functions help you avoid repeating code and make your programs more organized.

def greet():
    print("Hello, welcome!")
    print("Let's learn Python.")

# Call the function
greet()
greet()  # Can be called multiple times

Functions with Parameters

def greet(name):
    print(f"Hello, {name}!")

greet("Alice")  # Hello, Alice!
greet("Bob")    # Hello, Bob!

topics.title: Return Values
topics.content:
Returning Data from Functions

Functions can compute a value and return it to the caller using the return keyword:

def add(a, b):
    return a + b

result = add(5, 3)
print(result)  # 8

# You can use the return value directly
print(add(10, 20))  # 30

Multiple Return Values

def min_max(numbers):
    return min(numbers), max(numbers)

lo, hi = min_max([4, 1, 9, 2, 7])
print(lo, hi)  # 1 9

Default Parameters

def power(base, exponent=2):
    return base ** exponent

print(power(3))     # 9  (uses default exponent)
print(power(3, 3))  # 27 (overrides default)

topics.title: Scope — Local vs Global
topics.content:
Variable Scope

Scope refers to where a variable can be accessed. Variables created inside a function are local — they only exist within that function.

def my_func():
    local_var = 10      # Only accessible inside my_func
    print(local_var)    # Works fine

my_func()
# print(local_var)    # NameError! Not accessible here

Global Variables

counter = 0  # Global variable

def increment():
    global counter     # Tell Python to use the global one
    counter += 1

increment()
increment()
print(counter)  # 2

💡 Tip: Avoid overusing global. Prefer passing values as parameters and returning results.

evalQuestions.question: What keyword defines a function in Python?

What does this function return?
```
def double(x):
    return x * 2
double(6)
```
A variable created inside a function is called a ___ variable.
What keyword is used to send a value back from a function?

topics.title: Lists
topics.content:
Working with Lists

A list is an ordered, mutable collection of items. Lists can hold any data type.

fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]
numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
mixed = [42, "hello", True, 3.14]

# Access by index (starts at 0)
print(fruits[0])   # apple
print(fruits[-1])  # cherry (last item)

# Slicing
print(fruits[1:3]) # ['banana', 'cherry']

Modifying Lists

colors = ["red", "green", "blue"]

colors.append("yellow")      # Add to end
colors.insert(1, "orange")   # Insert at index 1
colors.remove("green")       # Remove by value
popped = colors.pop()        # Remove and return last item

print(colors)
print(len(colors))  # Length of list

topics.title: List Methods & Sorting
topics.content:
Useful List Methods

nums = [3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9, 2, 6]

nums.sort()          # Sort in place: [1, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 9]
nums.reverse()       # Reverse in place
print(nums.count(1)) # Count occurrences: 2
print(nums.index(5)) # Find index of 5
nums.clear()         # Empty the list

List Comprehension Preview

# Traditional way
squares = []
for i in range(1, 6):
    squares.append(i ** 2)

# List comprehension (much cleaner)
squares = [i ** 2 for i in range(1, 6)]
print(squares)  # [1, 4, 9, 16, 25]

topics.title: Tuples
topics.content:
What is a Tuple?

A tuple is like a list but immutable — you cannot change it after creation. Use parentheses ():

point = (3, 7)      # x, y coordinates
rgb = (255, 128, 0) # Red, Green, Blue
name_age = ("Alice", 25)

print(point[0])  # 3
print(len(rgb))  # 3

# Tuples are immutable
# point[0] = 10  # TypeError!

When to Use Tuple vs List?

  • Use tuples for fixed data (coordinates, RGB values, database records)
  • Use lists for data that needs to change (shopping cart, student roster)
# Tuple unpacking
x, y = (10, 20)
print(x)  # 10
print(y)  # 20

evalQuestions.question: Which method adds an item to the end of a list?
What is the index of the first element in a Python list?
What makes a tuple different from a list?
What does `nums[-1]` return?

topics.title: Dictionaries
topics.content:
Key-Value Storage

A dictionary stores data as key-value pairs. Keys must be unique and immutable (strings or numbers).

student = {
    "name": "Alice",
    "age": 20,
    "grade": "A",
    "enrolled": True
}

# Access values by key
print(student["name"])    # Alice
print(student.get("age")) # 20 (safer — returns None if missing)

# Add / update
student["score"] = 95
student["grade"] = "A+"

# Delete
del student["enrolled"]

topics.title: Dictionary Methods
topics.content:
Useful Dictionary Methods

person = {"name": "Bob", "age": 30, "city": "Manila"}

print(person.keys())    # dict_keys(['name', 'age', 'city'])
print(person.values())  # dict_values(['Bob', 30, 'Manila'])
print(person.items())   # dict_items([('name', 'Bob'), ...])

# Loop through a dictionary
for key, value in person.items():
    print(f"{key}: {value}")

Nested Dictionaries

classroom = {
    "students": {
        "Alice": {"score": 90},
        "Bob":   {"score": 85}
    },
    "subject": "Python"
}

print(classroom["students"]["Alice"]["score"])  # 90

topics.title: Sets
topics.content:
What is a Set?

A set is an unordered collection of unique values. Sets automatically remove duplicates.

colors = {"red", "green", "blue", "red"}
print(colors)  # {'red', 'green', 'blue'} — no duplicate!

# Add and remove
colors.add("yellow")
colors.discard("green")

# Set operations
a = {1, 2, 3, 4}
b = {3, 4, 5, 6}

print(a | b)  # Union:        {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6}
print(a & b)  # Intersection: {3, 4}
print(a - b)  # Difference:   {1, 2}

Sets are great for removing duplicates from a list:

names = ["Ana", "Bob", "Ana", "Cara", "Bob"]
unique = list(set(names))
print(unique)  # ['Ana', 'Bob', 'Cara']

evalQuestions.question: How do you access the value for key "name" in a dictionary `d`?
What does a set guarantee about its elements?
Which dictionary method returns all key-value pairs as tuples?
What symbol is used for the intersection of two sets?

topics.title: Common String Methods
topics.content:
String Manipulation

Strings in Python have many built-in methods for manipulation:

text = "  Hello, Python World!  "

print(text.strip())       # Remove leading/trailing spaces
print(text.lower())       # Convert to lowercase
print(text.upper())       # Convert to uppercase
print(text.title())       # Capitalize Each Word
print(text.replace("Python", "Amazing"))  # Replace substring
print(text.count("l"))    # Count occurrences: 3
print(text.find("Python")) # Index of substring: 9

Splitting and Joining

sentence = "the quick brown fox"
words = sentence.split()  # Split by whitespace
print(words)              # ['the', 'quick', 'brown', 'fox']

joined = "-".join(words)  # Join with separator
print(joined)             # the-quick-brown-fox

topics.title: String Formatting
topics.content:
f-strings (Recommended)

f-strings let you embed expressions directly inside string literals:

name = "Alice"
score = 95.5

print(f"Hello, {name}!")
print(f"Score: {score:.1f}%")  # 1 decimal place
print(f"2 + 2 = {2 + 2}")
print(f"Name length: {len(name)}")

Other Formatting Methods

# .format() method
print("Hello, {}! You scored {}".format(name, score))

# Named placeholders
print("{name} got {score}%".format(name="Bob", score=88))

# Alignment and padding
print(f"{name:>10}")   # Right-aligned in 10 chars
print(f"{score:08.2f}") # Zero-padded float

topics.title: String Slicing & Checking
topics.content:
Slicing Strings

You can extract parts of a string using slicing, just like lists:

text = "Hello, World!"

print(text[0:5])    # Hello
print(text[7:])     # World!
print(text[-6:])    # World!
print(text[::2])    # HloWrd  (every 2nd char)
print(text[::-1])   # !dlroW ,olleH (reversed)

Checking String Content

s = "Python3"

print(s.startswith("Py"))  # True
print(s.endswith("3"))     # True
print("on" in s)            # True
print(s.isdigit())          # False
print("123".isdigit())      # True
print("hello".isalpha())    # True
print("Hello World".isalnum())  # False (space!)

evalQuestions.question: What does `"hello".upper()` return?
Which f-string correctly embeds a variable `x = 5`?
What does `"a,b,c".split(",")` return?
What does `text[::-1]` do to a string?

topics.title: Opening & Reading Files
topics.content:
Working with Files

Python makes it easy to read from and write to files using the built-in open() function.

Reading a File

# Open a file for reading ('r' is default mode)
file = open("data.txt", "r")
content = file.read()       # Read entire file
print(content)
file.close()                # Always close!

# Read line by line
file = open("data.txt", "r")
for line in file:
    print(line.strip())
file.close()

Read Methods

f = open("notes.txt", "r")
all_text  = f.read()         # Entire file as one string
first_line = f.readline()    # One line at a time
all_lines = f.readlines()    # List of all lines
f.close()

topics.title: Writing Files & Context Manager
topics.content:
Writing to Files

# 'w' mode: creates or overwrites the file
f = open("output.txt", "w")
f.write("Hello, File!\n")
f.write("Second line.\n")
f.close()

# 'a' mode: append to existing file
f = open("output.txt", "a")
f.write("Appended line.\n")
f.close()

The with Statement (Best Practice)

The with statement automatically closes the file — even if an error occurs:

with open("data.txt", "r") as f:
    content = f.read()
    print(content)
# File is automatically closed here

# Writing with context manager
with open("result.txt", "w") as f:
    for i in range(5):
        f.write(f"Line {i + 1}\n")

💡 Always use with — it's cleaner and safer than manual close().

evalQuestions.question: Which mode opens a file for appending without deleting existing content?
What is the advantage of using `with open(...) as f`?
Which method reads all lines of a file and returns a list?
What mode do you use to overwrite a file completely?

topics.title: Classes & Objects
topics.content:
What is OOP?

Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) organizes code around objects — real-world entities that have attributes (data) and methods (behavior).

class Dog:
    # Class attribute (shared by all instances)
    species = "Canis familiaris"

    # Constructor method
    def __init__(self, name, age):
        self.name = name    # Instance attribute
        self.age  = age

    # Method
    def bark(self):
        return f"{self.name} says: Woof!"

# Create objects (instances)
dog1 = Dog("Rex", 3)
dog2 = Dog("Bella", 5)

print(dog1.bark())     # Rex says: Woof!
print(dog2.name)       # Bella
print(Dog.species)     # Canis familiaris

topics.title: Inheritance
topics.content:
Inheritance

Inheritance lets a new class (child) reuse and extend the code of an existing class (parent).

class Animal:
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name

    def speak(self):
        return "Some sound"

    def __str__(self):
        return f"Animal: {self.name}"

class Cat(Animal):   # Cat inherits from Animal
    def speak(self): # Override parent method
        return f"{self.name} says: Meow!"

class Duck(Animal):
    def speak(self):
        return f"{self.name} says: Quack!"

animals = [Cat("Whiskers"), Duck("Donald")]
for a in animals:
    print(a.speak())

topics.title: Encapsulation & Properties
topics.content:
Encapsulation

Encapsulation hides internal data and provides controlled access through methods. Use a leading underscore _ for "private" attributes (by convention).

class BankAccount:
    def __init__(self, balance):
        self._balance = balance   # "private" attribute

    def deposit(self, amount):
        if amount > 0:
            self._balance += amount

    def withdraw(self, amount):
        if 0 < amount <= self._balance:
            self._balance -= amount
            return True
        return False

    @property
    def balance(self):      # Getter
        return self._balance

acc = BankAccount(1000)
acc.deposit(500)
print(acc.balance)  # 1500  (read-only via property)

evalQuestions.question: What is the name of the special method called when creating a new object?
In `class Cat(Animal):`, what is `Animal`?
What does `self` refer to in a class method?
What concept allows a child class to provide its own version of a parent's method?

topics.title: try / except
topics.content:
Handling Errors Gracefully

Instead of letting your program crash, you can catch errors using try/except:

try:
    number = int(input("Enter a number: "))
    result = 10 / number
    print(f"10 / {number} = {result}")
except ValueError:
    print("That's not a valid number!")
except ZeroDivisionError:
    print("Cannot divide by zero!")
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Unexpected error: {e}")

else and finally

try:
    f = open("data.txt", "r")
except FileNotFoundError:
    print("File not found!")
else:
    content = f.read()   # Runs only if no exception
    print(content)
finally:
    print("Always runs — great for cleanup!")

topics.title: Raising & Custom Exceptions
topics.content:
Raising Exceptions

You can deliberately raise exceptions using the raise keyword:

def set_age(age):
    if age < 0:
        raise ValueError("Age cannot be negative!")
    if age > 150:
        raise ValueError("Age seems unrealistic.")
    return age

try:
    set_age(-5)
except ValueError as e:
    print(f"Error: {e}")

Custom Exception Classes

class InsufficientFundsError(Exception):
    def __init__(self, amount, balance):
        self.amount  = amount
        self.balance = balance
        super().__init__(f"Cannot withdraw {amount}. Balance: {balance}")

def withdraw(balance, amount):
    if amount > balance:
        raise InsufficientFundsError(amount, balance)
    return balance - amount

try:
    withdraw(100, 200)
except InsufficientFundsError as e:
    print(e)

evalQuestions.question: Which block always executes, regardless of whether an exception occurred?
What exception is raised when you divide by zero?
What keyword deliberately triggers an exception?
Custom exception classes should inherit from which base class?

topics.title: List & Dict Comprehensions
topics.content:
List Comprehensions

A concise way to build lists using a single line:

# Traditional loop
squares = []
for x in range(1, 6):
    squares.append(x ** 2)

# List comprehension
squares = [x ** 2 for x in range(1, 6)]
print(squares)  # [1, 4, 9, 16, 25]

# With condition (filter)
evens = [x for x in range(10) if x % 2 == 0]
print(evens)    # [0, 2, 4, 6, 8]

Dict Comprehensions

words = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]
lengths = {w: len(w) for w in words}
print(lengths)  # {'apple': 5, 'banana': 6, 'cherry': 6}

# Squares dict
square_map = {x: x**2 for x in range(1, 6)}
print(square_map)  # {1: 1, 2: 4, 3: 9, 4: 16, 5: 25}

topics.title: Lambda, map & filter
topics.content:
Lambda Functions

A lambda is an anonymous one-line function:

# Regular function
def double(x):
    return x * 2

# Equivalent lambda
double = lambda x: x * 2
print(double(5))  # 10

# Multi-argument lambda
add = lambda a, b: a + b
print(add(3, 7))  # 10

map() and filter()

numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]

# map: apply function to each element
doubled = list(map(lambda x: x * 2, numbers))
print(doubled)  # [2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12]

# filter: keep elements where function returns True
evens = list(filter(lambda x: x % 2 == 0, numbers))
print(evens)    # [2, 4, 6]

# Sorting with lambda key
students = [("Alice", 90), ("Bob", 85), ("Cara", 92)]
students.sort(key=lambda s: s[1], reverse=True)
print(students)  # [('Cara', 92), ('Alice', 90), ('Bob', 85)]

topics.title: Generators & yield
topics.content:
What is a Generator?

A generator is a function that produces values one at a time using yield, instead of returning a list. It saves memory for large datasets.

def count_up(limit):
    n = 1
    while n <= limit:
        yield n       # Pause and return n
        n += 1

gen = count_up(5)
print(next(gen))  # 1
print(next(gen))  # 2

# Iterate with for loop
for num in count_up(5):
    print(num)    # 1 2 3 4 5

Generator Expressions

# Like list comprehension but with ()
gen_expr = (x ** 2 for x in range(1000000))  # Memory-efficient!

# Only computes values as needed
print(next(gen_expr))  # 0
print(next(gen_expr))  # 1

# Using sum with generator
total = sum(x ** 2 for x in range(100))
print(total)  # 328350

evalQuestions.question: What does `[x**2 for x in range(4)]` produce?
What keyword is used in a generator function instead of return?
What does `lambda x: x * 3` do?
options: Creates an anonymous function that triples x
evalQuestions.correctAnswer: Creates an anonymous function that triples x

evalQuestions.question: Which built-in function applies a function to every element in an iterable?

topics.title: Importing Modules
topics.content:
What is a Module?

A module is a Python file containing reusable code (functions, classes, variables). Python comes with a huge standard library of built-in modules.

import math
import random
import datetime

print(math.pi)           # 3.141592653589793
print(math.sqrt(16))     # 4.0
print(math.ceil(2.3))    # 3

print(random.randint(1, 10))   # Random int 1–10
print(random.choice(["a", "b", "c"]))  # Random item

today = datetime.date.today()
print(today)  # e.g. 2026-06-10

Import Styles

import math                    # Use as math.sqrt()
from math import sqrt          # Use as sqrt() directly
from math import sqrt, pi      # Multiple items
from math import *             # Import everything (avoid)
import numpy as np             # Alias (common for libraries)

topics.title: Creating Your Own Module
topics.content:
Writing a Module

Any Python file is a module. Create utils.py:

# utils.py
PI = 3.14159

def circle_area(radius):
    return PI * radius ** 2

def greet(name):
    return f"Hello, {name}!"

if __name__ == "__main__":
    # This runs only when utils.py is executed directly
    print(greet("World"))

Then import it in another file:

# main.py
import utils

print(utils.greet("Alice"))     # Hello, Alice!
print(utils.circle_area(5))     # 78.53975

The __name__ Guard

if __name__ == "__main__": ensures test code only runs when the file is executed directly, not when imported.

evalQuestions.question: Which import statement lets you use `sqrt(16)` directly (without `math.`)?
What does `if __name__ == '__main__':` do?
options: Runs code only when the file is executed directly
Checks if the module is the main library
evalQuestions.correctAnswer: Runs code only when the file is executed directly

evalQuestions.question: Which module provides `randint()` for generating random integers?
What alias is conventionally used for the `numpy` library?
